coredata.dc:title: Electrodeposition one-step preparation of silver nanoparticles/carbon dots/reduced graphene oxide ternary dendritic nanocomposites for sensitive detection of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Huijun
Jin, Hui
Gui, Rijun
Wang, Zonghua
Xia, Jianfei
Zhang, Feifei
coredata.dc:description: In this article, ternary nanocomposites consisting of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), carbon dots (CDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) were newly synthesized via a one-step electrodeposition method. Upon cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning, ternary nanocomposites with dendritic structure were facilely generated and electrodeposited on glass carbon electrode (GCE), without involving toxic solvents and reducing agents. The nanocomposites were characterized by means of scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) techniques. Characterization results revealed that the nanocomposites showed dendritic structures and dispersible narrow-sized nanoparticles were deposited on the matrix of rGO, mainly attributed to the use of Ag(NH3)2OH instead of Ag(NO3)2 as the precursor. The nanocomposites deposited on GCE had superior electrocatalytic activities for doxorubicin (DOX) reduction. The electrocatalytic activities were strongly affected by Ag(NH3)2OH concentration. The best electrocatalytic activities could be obtained when the volume ratio of CDs-GO (3:1, v/v) to Ag(NH3)2OH was fixed to 1:1. The peak current intensities of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system linearly increased upon the increase of coexisting DOX concentration in the range from 1.0×10−8 to 2.5×10−6 M (R 2 =0.9956), together with a limit of detection as low as 2nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrodeposition
Reduced graphene oxide
Nanocomposites
Doxorubicin
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical responses of agNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE on DOX
Selectivity, repeatability and stability of the sensing system
Detection of DOX in real biological samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation and application of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system.
XRD patterns of the as-synthesized AgNPs-CDs-rGO ternary nanocomposites.
SEM images (a-c) and TEM images (d-g) of the AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites.

Cyclic voltammograms of GCE modified with (a) rGO, (b) AgNPs-rGO complex and (c) AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites. (d) Cyclic voltammograms of AgNPs-CDs-rGO modified GCE under different volume ratios of CDs to GO: (a) 3:1; (b) 1:1; (c) 1:3 and (d) bare GCE. CV (e) and DPV (f) electrochemical response curves of the AgNPs-CDs-rGO modified GCE in the presence of DOX from 1×10−13 to 1×10−4 M.

(a) DPV curves of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system in 1mM PBS (pH 7.0) with the addition of DOX at increasing concentrations from 1×10−8 to 1×10−7, 5×10−7, 7.5×10−7, 1×10−6 and 2.5×10−6 M. (b) Linear relationship between the current peak intensity (Ip) of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE and the coexisting DOX concentration.

Peak current intensities of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE in the presence of DOX (a), DOX plus bovine serum albumin (b), DOX plus lysozyme (c), DOX plus glucose oxidase (d), DOX plus 5-fluorouracil (e), DOX plus methotrexate (f), bovine serum albumin (g), lysozyme (h), glucose oxidase (i),5-fluorouracil (j) and methotrexate (k). The concentration of each type of molecules was fixed to 2.5μM.
Determination of DOX in real human serum samples based on the as-synthesized sensing system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Samples were prepared by diluting real human serum samples for 50-fold with PBS.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All detected results were expressed as the average of six repeated determinations±standard deviation (SD).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (RSD) was defined as (SD/mean)×100%.

head.ce:title: Electrodeposition one-step preparation of silver nanoparticles/carbon dots/reduced graphene oxide ternary dendritic nanocomposites for sensitive detection of doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University, Shandong 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials
Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile
The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Two authors have the equivalent contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, ternary nanocomposites consisting of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), carbon dots (CDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) were newly synthesized via a one-step electrodeposition method. Upon cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning, ternary nanocomposites with dendritic structure were facilely generated and electrodeposited on glass carbon electrode (GCE), without involving toxic solvents and reducing agents. The nanocomposites were characterized by means of scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) techniques. Characterization results revealed that the nanocomposites showed dendritic structures and dispersible narrow-sized nanoparticles were deposited on the matrix of rGO, mainly attributed to the use of Ag(NH3)2OH instead of Ag(NO3)2 as the precursor. The nanocomposites deposited on GCE had superior electrocatalytic activities for doxorubicin (DOX) reduction. The electrocatalytic activities were strongly affected by Ag(NH3)2OH concentration. The best electrocatalytic activities could be obtained when the volume ratio of CDs-GO (3:1, v/v) to Ag(NH3)2OH was fixed to 1:1. The peak current intensities of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system linearly increased upon the increase of coexisting DOX concentration in the range from 1.0×10−8 to 2.5×10−6 M (R 2 =0.9956), together with a limit of detection as low as 2nM.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (DOX), as one of important anthracycline antibiotics, has been extensively applied in the treatment of several categories of cancers, such as tissue and osteogenic sarcoma, soft breast and thyroid, lymphoblastic leukemia, and so forth [1,2]. As established, the use of DOX at a high dose is closely associated with the therapy of cardiomyopathies and myelo-suppression [3]. Most serious effects of DOX on human body can result in life-threatening heart damage [4]. Owing to above characteristics of DOX, it is meaningful to develop a simple and efficient analytical method for DOX detection. Especially, the detection of DOX in biomedical samples is significant, which potentially serves as a reliable indicator for the monitoring of human body health factors, diagnose and therapy of several relative cancers [5].

So far, many conventional analytical technologies have been developed in succession and used for qualitative or quantitative DOX detection, including high performance liquid chromatography [6–17], liquid chromatography and capillary electrophoresis [18,19], UV–vis absorption spectrum [20], fluorescence emission spectrum [21–23], electrochemical analysis [24–28], etc. Conventional instrument analytical technologies for the determination of DOX generally have some limitations, such as complicated preconcentration, time-consuming operation and low sensitivity. By contrast, electrochemical analysis methods especially electrochemical sensors have unique advantages and received tremendous attention, which are mainly due to the superior electroactivity of quinone and hydroxiquinone groups of DOX. The most obvious advantages of electrochemical sensors include simplicity, low cost and high sensitivity, and the electrochemical sensor of DOX has been reported in previous researches [29–40].

In the process of electrochemical sensing, the electron transfer rate becomes very slow when unmodified electrodes are used for DOX sensing. On this account, some modified electrodes have been exploited towards the electrochemical sensing of DOX. For instance, recently nano-TiO2/Nafion composite film modified electrodes [41], and β-cyclodextrin-graphene hybrid nanosheets modified GCE [42] have been developed. The use of modified electrodes was expected to realize the improvement of electron transfer rate and sensitivity in the sensing process of DOX. In order to further improve electron transfer rate and sensitivity for electrochemical sensing applications, the selection of carbon nanomaterials or noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) seems very necessary. When electrode interface was modified with different substances containing carbon nanomaterials (graphene oxide, multi-walled carbon nanotube or carbon dots) or noble metal NPs (gold or silver NPs), larger active surface area and superior electron transfer efficiency on the modified electrode interface could be obtained rationally. In earlier reports, carbon nanomaterials modified electrodes such as the composite film (containing magnetic graphene oxide (GO) grafted with chlorosulfonic acid, Fe3O4-GO-SO3H) modified electrode [43], graphene quantum dots modified electrode [44], and oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) modified electrode [45] were constructed to improve the electrochemical sensing efficiency of DOX.

In previous reports, the electrochemical sensing of DOX has been developed based on modified electrodes. As summarized in Supporting Information (Table S1), the interface of electrodes was generally modified with different substances containing single species of carbon nanomaterials or noble NPs. However, when the substances that were used to modify electrode interface involved the combination of carbon nanomaterials and noble NPs, the synergist effect from different species of electroactive nanomaterials could be achieved reasonably [45–51]. Potentially, combination of electroactive nanomaterials on electrode interface could lead to the improvement of conductivity, the increase of surface areas and active sites, and so dramatically accelerate electron transfer rate. When substances containing multiple electroactive nanomaterials were used to modify electrode interface to construct sensing system, multiple-amplifying electrochemical signal response would be obtained and suitable for highly sensitive detection of analytes.

Herein, ternary nanocomposites consisting of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), carbon dots (CDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) were firstly prepared via a one-step electrodeposition method. Through cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning, ternary nanocomposites with dendritic structure were facilely produced and electrodeposited on glass carbon electrode (GCE), without the use of toxic solvents and reducing agents. Since the nanocomposites-modified GCE sensing system contained multiple electroactive nanomaterials, its multiple-amplifying electrochemical signal responses on electroactive targets (e.g. DOX) would be generated. Hence, the sensing system could be further developed for highly sensitive determination of targets. According to the unique characteristics of this as-synthesized one-step electrodeposition method for preparation of novel dendritic ternary nanocomposites (e.g., simplicity, low cost, non-toxicity and high efficiency), this as-synthesized method would be promising. In this work, the ternary nanocomposites were deposited on GCE to construct sensing system, which possessed superior electrocatalytic activities for DOX reduction. Highly sensitive electrochemical signal responses to DOX could be detected. Owing to the almost linear relationship between the peak current intensities and coexisting DOX concentration [DOX], this as-synthesized AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system had a significant potential for highly sensitive detection of DOX (Scheme 1 ).

DOX was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. China. AgNO3 (99.7%), ammonia solution (25wt%), H2SO4 (98%), HNO3 (98%) and other chemicals with the analytical grade were bought from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. All reagents and chemicals can be directly used as received without further purification. Doubly distilled water is required to use in all experiments. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solutions were prepared by adjusting the concentration ratio of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. GO was prepared from a simply modified version of Hummers’ method [52]. The aqueous suspension of the as-prepared GO was dialyzed to remove probably extra acids and salts, followed by centrifugation and drying treatments. More details are available in Supporting Information (Part S1). CDs were facilely prepared based on the reported method [53], through one-pot hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and NH4HCO3. Relative experimental details are available in Part S2. Real human serum samples used in this work were collected from a healthy young volunteer and purchased from a local hospital.

Electrochemical measurements were performed with a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China). A conventional three-electrode system consisted of AgNPs-CDs-rGO ternary nanocomposites-modified GCE as the working electrode, a potassium chloride-saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a Pt wire electrode as the auxiliary electrode. Different electrochemical measurement modes, both cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), were utilized in experiments. All electrochemical measurements were performed repeatedly. The each result was expressed as the average from six repeated measurements. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Siemens) patterns were measured with a D5005 X-ray powder diffractometer with graphite monochromatized Cu K α radiation (λ =1.54056Å). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded on JEOL JSM-6700F SEM, while transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from JEOL JEM-2100 TEM with an acceleration voltage of 120kV.

To prepare silver-ammonia Ag(NH3)2OH solution, 1wt% of ammonia solution was added in 50mM of AgNO3 solution until the precipitates were invisible visually by naked eye. Concentration of the resulting Ag(NH3)2OH was adjusted to 40mM. Aqueous suspensions of CDs (1.0mgml−1) and GO (1.0mgml−1) were prepared and placed in separate containers. By regulating the solution volume ratio of CDs and GO to 3:1, the mixture solution of CDs-GO was obtained under stirring for 30min at room temperature. Afterward, a freshly polished GCE with mirror-like surface was immersed into deoxygenated PBS (1mM, pH 7.0). CDs-GO mixture solution was added into the PBS electrolyte solution, followed by the addition of Ag(NH3)2OH solution. The volume ratio of CDs-GO and Ag(NH3)2OH was fixed to 1:1. Suffering from CV scanning at a rate of 25mVs−1 in a potential range of 0–1.0V, the reductive reaction on the interface of GCE immersed into PBS electrolyte solution containing CDs-GO and Ag(NH3)2OH occurred [54]. After CV scanning for several cycles, AgNPs-CDs-rGO ternary nanocomposites were generated and electrodeposited on GCE interface, indicating the formation of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE.

Electrochemical measurements of different samples were performed with a conventional three- electrode electrochemical workstation. The prepared AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system was immersed in PBS (1mM, pH 7.0) electrolyte solution without or with the coexistence of DOX at different concentrations, followed by the electrochemical measurements of CV and DPV modes. As a reference, cyclic voltammograms of rGO/GCE and AgNPs-rGO/GCE from electrodeposition reaction on the interface of GCE were also measured. During the electrodeposition reaction, cyclic voltammograms of modified GCE were evaluated at different solution volume ratios of CDs-GO complex to Ag(NH3)2OH. Cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE in PBS with the addition of DOX at an increased coexisting concentration was measured. DPV responses of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system were recorded under DOX coexisting conditions in PBS. Potential interferences from biological fluids (e.g., glucose, lactose, ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid) were selected and added into PBS electrolyte solution. DPV responses of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE immersed into the mixed PBS electrolyte solution were measured to evaluate the selective DPV responses of this sensing system on DOX. In PBS electrolyte solution mixed with 50-fold diluted human serum samples with the addition of DOX at different coexisting concentrations, a series of DPV curves of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE were measured. In view of the plotting linear relationship between peak current intensity and [DOX], [DOX] in real biological fluids could be calculated after achievement of the according DPV peak current intensities.

In the overall steps, experiments relative to the independent electrodeposition, preparation and voltammetric measurements were conducted repeatedly. The corresponding results were expressed as the average of six parallel experiments.

XRD patterns of the as-prepared AgNPs-CDs-rGO ternary nanocomposites were characterized as shown in Fig. 1 . Clearly, the disappearance of a typical diffraction peak of GO at ∼10° and the appearance of a diffraction peak at ∼22° indicated the reductive reaction from GO to rGO [36,55]. A broad amorphous peak appeared around ∼25°, which was mainly due to the coexistence of CDs. Diffraction peak intensities for typical 111, 200, 220 and 311 of Ag (Ref. code: 003-0921) could be observed remarkably, which revealed that AgNPs had been generated during electrodeposition process. Other diffraction peaks were probably ascribed to the substance materials that were used to sustain AgNPs-CDs-rGO for XRD characterization. The intensities of GO-related oxygen peaks decreased [55] and the typical diffraction peak of GO disappeared. These results were attributed to the growth of AgNPs on the surface of graphene nanosheets, which could prevent the restacking of graphene nanosheets efficiently [26]. Meanwhile, the delocalized conjugation was restored in rGO sample. Consequently, these results mentioned above demonstrated the successful preparation of AgNPs-CDs-rGO ternary nanocomposites.

The morphology of AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites was observed by SEM and TEM images. Fig. 2 a-c showed the SEM images of nanocomposites under different magnification times and in different visual fields. Markedly, specific nanomaterials with the dendritic structure were found. In Fig. 2d, the dendritic structure was obviously observed from the TEM images of nanocomposites. Under high magnification times, the branches and trunks of dendritic structures could be distinctly seen in Fig. 2e and Fig. 2f. Based upon the preparation strategy of AgNPs-CDs-rGO, dispersible nanoparticles (AgNPs, CDs) would be generated through reductive reaction and electrodeposited on the matrix of rGO. The trunk structure should be constructed from rGO matrix. The flake-like curved structure of rGO served as a sustaining platform for the deposition of nanoparticles. When the volume ratio of CDs-GO (3:1, v/v) to Ag(NH3)2OH reached to 1:1, a reductive reaction of Ag ions occurred and AgNPs were generated accordingly. There, partial AgNPs were deposited along the branch direction of rGO. Due to the deposition of nanoparticles in branch and trunk directions, the dendritic structure of nanoparticles-deposited rGO could be fabricated favorably. As shown in Fig. 2g, the larger and smaller nanoparticles were assigned to AgNPs and CDs, respectively. In the formation process of AgNPs, alkaline Ag(NH3)2OH precursors could be absorbed by negatively charged CDs and GO through neutralization, facilitating the creation of more initial nucleation sites, high stability and resistance to the reduction of Ag(NH3)2OH [56]. These properties ensured to form AgNPs with almost uniform sizes and to prevent the growth of AgNPs into large particles [24]. In Fig. 2e, some silver agglomerations were found on the surface of dendritic structures, and had the combination of CDs and GO to form AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites.

In order to characterize the electrochemical properties of GCE modified with AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites, a series of cyclic voltammograms were measured regularly. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a–c, the peak current intensities had a gradual decrease with the increase of CV scanning cycle numbers (1–5), which suggested the formation of electroactive nanomaterials on GCE interface. When compared with AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites-modified GCE (Fig. 3c), rGO (Fig. 3a) or AgNPs-rGO complex (Fig. 3b) modified GCE exhibited relatively low peak current intensities under the same CV scanning conditions (scanning rate of 25mVs−1, potential range of −1.5–0V). The superior peak current in Fig. 3c was mainly attributed to the combination of AgNPs-CDs-rGO three components in nanocomposites. The combination could trigger a potential synergistic effect to significantly improve the electrochemical signal responses on the interface of nanocomposites- modified GCE. Notably, AgNPs, CDs and rGO together were used for the coexisting components of substances to modify GCE, which would efficiently promote the conductivity and increase the surface areas and active sites for electron transfer.

In addition, the optimal conditions for AgNPs-CDs-rGO preparation were studied. We prepared the CDs-GO complex solution through adjusting the solution volume ratio of CDs to GO. Both the concentrations of CDs and GO were fixed to 1.0mgml−1 identically, while the ratio of CDs-GO complex total volume to Ag(NH3)2OH (40mM) volume was approximately 1:1. At the solution volume ratio of CDs to GO changing from 3:1 to 1:1 and 1:3, three samples of AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites were prepared through reductive reaction and electrodeposition on GCE interface accordingly. As exhibited in Fig. 3d, the peak current intensity from sample “a” with a volume ratio of 3:1 was apparently higher than that from sample “b” and “c” with the volume ratios of 1:1 and 1:3, respectively. Due to the achievement of high peak current intensity, the optimal solution volume ratio of CDs to GO should be 3:1. In other words, AgNPs-CDs-rGO nanocomposites when prepared under the optimal conditions and deposited on electrode interface could possess superior electrochemical properties [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical responses of agNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE on DOX
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the superior electroactivity of quinone and hydroxiquinone groups of DOX molecules, it is meaningful to study the electrochemical responses of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system on DOX. On the interface of GCE with modification of electroactive substances, DOX reduction can occur through electrocatalytic reaction. In this work, AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE was immersed in the PBS electrolyte solution with the coexistence of DOX at different concentrations [DOX]. A series of CV and DPV electrochemical response curves from the AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system containing DOX were measured. In Fig. 3e, CV curves with the gradually increased peak current intensities were observed. Peak current intensities of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE regularly increased with the gradual increase of coexisting [DOX]. DOX at a high coexisting concentration endowed an improved electrochemical signal response of this sensing system in CV mode. Participation of more DOX molecules induced much stronger electrochemical signal responses. Similar results could be achieved when DPV mode was also used for electrochemical curve measurements of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE in the presence of DOX (Fig. 3f). Results in Fig. 3e–f revealed superior electrochemical signal responses of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE on DOX, thus indicating a potential to develop a new electrochemical sensor for highly sensitive detection of DOX.

To reveal superior performance of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE for sensitive electrochemical sensing of DOX, bare GCE before and after modification with AgNPs-CDs-rGO was immersed in PBS containing DOX and measured by CV and DPV modes. When DOX concentration was as low as 1×10−7 M, the electrochemical curves measured by bare GCE could not discriminate with the case at 1×10−6 M of DOX (Fig. S1). This result meant that bare GCE was not sensitive to DOX at a concentration lower than 1×10−6 M. When DOX existed at a concentration from 1×10−6 to 1×10−8 M, the AgNPs-CDs-rGO modified GCE presented a gradually reduced and obviously differential electrochemical signal response to DOX (Fig. S2). It suggested that AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE had superior electrochemical signal responses over bare GCE, and enabled highly sensitive detection of DOX even at a low concentration of 1×10−8 M. By reverse and forward scanning in DPV mode, AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE was used to measure DOX in PBS. The oxidation peak current of DOX was higher than its reduction peak current (Fig. S3), and thus was selected as the response signal for electrochemical sensing of DOX in this work.

A new electrochemical sensor was developed based on AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system. In detail, AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE was immersed in 1mM PBS (pH 7.0) with the addition of DOX. When the coexisting concentration of DOX [DOX] increased from 1.0×10−8 to 2.5×10−6 M, there is an increase tendency in the peak current intensities through DPV electrochemical measurements of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE (Fig. 4 a). As demonstrated in Fig. 4b, the peak current intensities nearly linearly increased with the increase of [DOX]. Especially, a good linear relationship between the oxidation peak current intensity (I p) and [DOX] was plotted. The linear regression equation could be expressed as I p (μA)=2.197+8.293 [DOX] (10−8 M) (R2
=0.9956). Limit of detection (LOD) was approximately 2nM, calculated from DOX concentration when a ratio of signal to noise (S/N) is 3. Although conventional analytical technologies have been developed for the detection of DOX [18–23], some potential limitation (e.g. complicated preconcentration, time-consuming operation, low sensitivity, etc) may restrain the practical applications of these technologies. Electrochemical analysis methods possess unique advantages including simplicity, low cost and high sensitivity, and have used for DOX detection [29–40]. The linear range and LOD of this work are comparable with those of previously reported electrochemical methods for DOX detection as summarized in Supporting Information (Table S1). Among these previous reports, the interface of electrodes was generally modified with carbon nanomaterials or noble NPs. To improve electrochemical detection sensitivity, the combination carbon nanomaterials (CDs, rGO) and noble NPs (AgNPs) on GCE interface is significant because of the synergist effect from different electroactive nanomaterials. So far, there is still no report that refers to AgNPs-CDs-rGO ternary dendritic nanocomposites and the nanocomposites-modified GCE for DOX detection. Due to superiority of multiple-amplifying electrochemical responses, comparable linear detection range and LOD, AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE exhibited a significant potential for highly sensitive detection of DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability and stability of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of electrochemical signal responses of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system on DOX was evaluated by the mode of DPV. Potential interferences from biological systems or other anticancer drugs to the detection of DOX were selected, such as bovine serum albumin, lysozyme, glucose oxidase, 5-fluorouracil and methotrexate. The effects of these selected interferences as the competitors of DOX were evaluated in the following electrochemical sensing experiments. The peak current intensities of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE in the presence of DOX and interferences were measured respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 , each type of interferences only produced a negligible influence on peak current intensities, in comparison with DOX at the same existing concentration (2.5μM). When AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE was immersed in PBS with the coexistence of both each type of interferences and DOX, the peak current intensities were very close to the case with only existence of DOX without interferences. These results further implied that AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system possessed the analytical capability of highly selectively detect DOX, over potential interferences. In electrochemical measurements, a clean surface of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE was always retained after the each modified GCE was placed in dark at 4°C and covered with a beaker for 15days. The values of relative standard deviation (RSD) from six repeated measurements of peak current intensities were lower than 2.2%. Low RSD revealed that the sensing system had the high repeatability and stability, further suggesting a high feasibility to use AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system for DOX detection.

In the process of electrochemical sensing, DOX in PBS electrolyte solution was combined with AgNPs-CDs-rGO coating on the surface of GCE, mainly originating from electrostatic adsorption, hydrogen bond or intermolecular force. Moreover, marked electrochemical (CV) signal responses of commercial electroactive substances (such as 1×10−6 M of dopamine or uric acid) with partially similar molecular structures to DOX could be achieved in PBS, which further revealed the highly sensitive electrochemical sensing capacity of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DOX in real biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the feasibility of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system for DOX detection, a series of experiments were regularly conducted. Real human serum fluids obtained from a local hospital were 50-fold diluted with PBS to prepare real biological samples, without any further purification of samples. In the each group of human serum samples with the coexistence of DOX at different concentrations, the values of [DOX] were determined by AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE, according to the plotted linear relationship between the peak current intensity (Ip) and [DOX] in Fig. 4b. As clearly summarized in Table 1 , the measured [DOX] was in good agreement with spiked [DOX], together with high detection recoveries of 98.0–102.0%, low RSD values and narrow RSD distribution of 1.3–2.9% calculated from six repeated electrochemical measurements by means of DPV mode. High agreement between the measured and spiked values of [DOX], high detection recovery and low RSD powerfully testified the high reliability and applicability when AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE was used for DOX detection in real biological samples.

In conclusion, a new AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system that consisted of ternary dendritic nanocomposites (AgNPs-CDs-rGO) deposited on the GCE interface was constructed by means of a facile one-step electrodeposition method under CV scanning. The combination of electroactive CDs, rGO and AgNPs endowed an improved electrochemical signal of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE for highly sensitive sensing of targets. Experimental results indicated that AgNPs-CDs-rGO exhibited unique dendritic structures. Multi-amplifying electrochemical signal (CV and DPV) responses of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE on DOX coexisting in PBS electrolyte solution were obtained. There is a good linear relationship between the DPV peak current intensity (Ip) and [DOX] in the range from 1.0×10−8 to 2.5×10−6 M (R 2 =0.9956), with a low LOD of 2nM. The electrochemical responses of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE on DOX were highly sensitive and selective, over potential interferences. In real sample analysis, the AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system still showed superior analytical performance, accompanying with high detection recoveries, low RSD values and narrow RSD distribution. These results proved the high reliability and applicability of AgNPs-CDs-rGO/GCE sensing system for highly efficient DOX detection in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475071 and 21405086), the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong (ts201511027), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2014BQ001 and ZR2016BB37), and the Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao (17-1-1-72-jch).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.06.095.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step electrochemical approach for the preparation of graphene wrapped-phosphotungstic acid hybrid and its application for simultaneous determination of sunset yellow and tartrazine
Liposomes as Drug Carriers: Recent Trends and Progress
Recent developments in the field of antitumour anthracyclines
Effects of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor on delayed-onset doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity
Daunorubicin and doxorubicin anthracycline antibiotics, a physicochemical and biological review
HPLC methods for the determination of bound and free doxorubicin, and of bound and free galactosamine, in methacrylamide polymer-drug conjugates
HPLC-MS/MS determination of a peptide conjugate prodrug of doxorubicin and its active metabolites, leucine-doxorubicin and doxorubicin, in dog and rat plasma
Selective determination of doxorubicin and doxorubicinol in rat plasma by HPLC with photosensitization reaction followed by chemiluminescence detection
Rapid and sensitive HPLC method for the estimation of doxorubicin in dog blood–the silver nitrate artifact
Analysis of intracellular doxorubicin and its metabolites by ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography
Selective determination of quinones by high-performance liquid chromatography with on-line post column ultraviolet irradiation and peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence detection
Determination of doxorubicin in rabbit ocular tissues and pharmacokinetics after intravitreal injection of a single dose of doxorubicin-loaded poly-β-hydroxybutyrate microspheres
Rapid determination of PEGylated liposomal doxorubicin and its major metabolite in human plasma by ultraviolet-visible high-performance liquid chromatography
HPLC determination of doxorubicin, doxorubicinol and four aglycone metabolites in plasma of AIDS patients
Determination of adriamycin and aclacinomycin in plasma by high pressure liquid chromatography and spectrophot of luorometry
Determination of doxorubicin and its metabolites in rat serum and bile by LC: application to preclinical pharmacokinetic studies
Quantification of doxorubicin and metabolites in rat plasma and small volume tissue samples by liquid chromatography/electrospray tandem mass spectroscopy
Selective determination of the doxorubicin content of individual acidic organelles in impure subcellular fractions
Flow injection spectrophotometric determination of doxorubicin hydrochloride in urine samples
A fluorometric assay for daunomycin and adriamycin in animal tissues
Fluorescence assays and pharmacokinetic studies of 4′-deoxydoxorubicin and doxorubicin in organs of mice bearing solid tumors
Determination of doxorubicin in human plasma by excitation-emission matrix fluorescence and multi-way analysis
Anodic voltammetry and AFM imaging of picomoles of adriamycin adsorbed onto carbon surfaces
Voltammetric determination of trace doxorubicin at a nano-titania/nafion composite film modified electrode in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide
Differential pulse polarographic analysis of adriamycin in plasma
Electrochemical determination of adriamycin compounds in urine by preconcentration at carbon paste electrodes
Disposition of 14C-labelled 4'-epidoxorubicin and doxorubicin in the rat. A comparative study
A simple electrochemical approach to fabricate a glucose biosensor based on graphene-glucose oxidase biocomposite
Fluorescent graphene oxide composites synthesis and its biocompatibility study
ITO/gold nanoparticle/RGD peptide composites to enhance electrochemical signals and proliferation of human neural stem cells
A DNA biosensor based on the detection of doxorubicin-conjugated Ag nanoparticle labels using solid-state voltammetry
A novel sensitive doxorubicin impedimetric immunosensor based on a specific monoclonal antibody-gold nanoaprticle-sol-gel modified electrode
Voltammetric behavior of doxorubicin at a renewable silver-amalgam film electrode and its determination in human urine
Generation of a doxorubicin immunosensor based on a specific monoclonal antibody-nanogold-modified electrode
Synthesis and functionalization of magnetic nanoparticles with covalently bound electroactive compound doxorubicin
A new kinetic-mechanistic approach to elucidate electrooxidation of doxorubicin hydrochloride in unprocessed human fluids using magnetic graphene based nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode
Carbon paste electrode based on surface activation for trace adriamycin determination by a preconcentration and voltammetric method
Electrodepositing of copper nanowires on layered double hydroxide film modified glassy carbon electrode for the determination of doxorubicin
Application of a disposable doxorubicin sensor for direct determination of clinical drug concentration in patient blood
Synthesis of ultrastable Ag nanoplates/polyethylenimine-reduced graphene oxide and its Application as a versatile electrochemical sensor
Electrochemical sensor for ultrasensitive determination of doxorubicin and methotrexate based on β-cyclodextrin-graphene hybrid nanosheets
Room temperature in situ chemical synthesis of Fe3O4/graphene
Sensing of doxorubicin hydrochloride using graphene quantum dot modified glassy carbon electrode
Electrochemically oxidized multiwalled carbon nanotube/glassy carbon electrode as a probe for simultaneous determination of dopamine and doxorubicin in biological samples
One-step electrodeposition synthesis of silver- nanoparticle-decorated graphene on indium-tin-oxide for enzymeless hydrogen peroxide detection
One-pot reduction of graphene oxide at subzero temperatures
Synthetic methods and potential applications of transition metal dichalcogenide/graphene nanocomposites
One-pot hydrothermal route to fabricate nitrogen doped graphene/Ag-TiO2: Efficient charge separation and high-performance on-off-on switch system based photoelectrochemical biosensing
Silver nanoparticles anchored on nitrogen-doped graphene as a novel electrochemical biosensing platform with enhanced sensitivity for aptamer-based pesticide assay
Enhanced electrochemiluminescence sensing platform using nitrogen-doped graphene as a novel two-dimensional mat of silver nanoparticles
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Paper-like graphene-Ag composite films with enhanced mechanical and electrical properties
Simple room-temperature preparation of high-yield large-area graphene oxide
Stacking nature of graphene layers in carbon nanotubes and nanofibres
The use of nanoparticles in electroanalysis: an updated review
One-step hydrothermal green synthesis of silver nanoparticle-carbon nanotube reduced-graphene oxide composite and its application as hydrogen peroxide sensor

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Huijun Guo was born in 1992. Since 2015, she has been a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, China. Her research interests focus on the field of electrochemical sensors.

Hui Jin was born in 1983. She obtained her PhD from Tongji University, China. Now, she is a postdoctoral researcher in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, China. Her research interests mainly focus on the fields of fluorescent, electrochemical sensing and imaging applications.

Rijun Gui received his BS degree in 2005, and completed his MS degree in Applied Chemistry from Shanghai Ocean University in 2009. He then studied at East China University of Science and Technology to pursue a PhD degree in Physical Chemistry. After completing his PhD degree in 2012, he works as a postdoctoral research fellow at Shanghai Jiao Tong University. In 2014, he joined to Qingdao University, where he is currently an assistant professor. His main research interests focus on the synthesis, properties and applications of electrochemical and fluorescent nanomaterials.

Zonghua Wang received her BS degree in Applied Chemistry (1984) and her MS degree in Analytical Chemistry (1987) both from China University of Geosciences. Afterward, she joined to Qingdao University. After achievement of her PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Tsinghua University in 2004, she works as a senior visiting professor at Bayreuth University (2005–2006). Currently, she works as a distinguished professor at Qingdao University. Her research interests focus on the preparation of electrochemical, fluorescent nanomaterials for chem./bio-analysis and energy conversion.
Jianfei Xia is an associate professor. His research focuses on electro-analytical chemistry.
Feifei Zhang is an associate professor. Her research focuses on electro-analytical chemistry.
